medianHouseholdIncome.label: Median household income
medianHouseholdIncome.note: ±$5,196. Roughly 50% above the U.S. median; Indiana's wealthiest county.

metrics.label: Bridges in poor condition
metrics.source: FHWA National Bridge Inventory (modeled from Indiana 5.3% structurally deficient, adjusted for Hamilton County's newer, better-funded inventory)
metrics.note: Indiana statewide is 5.3% structurally deficient; Hamilton County's bridges are newer and better maintained, so estimated below the state figure.

metrics.label: Roads in poor ride quality
metrics.source: FHWA HPMS / INDOT (modeled; affluent, recently built suburban network)
metrics.note: Estimated low given the county's newer road stock and high local capital spending on streets.

metrics.label: Roundabout ratio
metrics.source: OpenStreetMap / City of Carmel (Carmel alone: 160+ roundabouts vs ~10 signals; countywide blended with Fishers, Westfield, Noblesville)
metrics.note: Carmel is the roundabout capital of the U.S. with 160+ roundabouts and only about a dozen signalized intersections (Carmel-only ratio approaches 0.9). Blended countywide with less-converted municipalities yields ~0.42, still far above the 0.30 'strong' anchor.

metrics.label: Traffic deaths
metrics.source: NHTSA FARS / County Health Rankings (modeled)
metrics.note: Hamilton County reported ~7.0 motor-vehicle crash deaths per 100k; roundabout conversion has sharply cut injury-crash severity.

metrics.label: Sidewalk coverage
metrics.source: OpenStreetMap (partial mapped sidewalk-km per road-km; field-confirmed high coverage)
metrics.note: Strong sidewalk and multi-use-path coverage; Monon Trail and extensive path network. OSM coverage is incomplete so this is a conservative partial estimate.

metrics.label: Park acres per 1,000 residents
metrics.source: Trust for Public Land / local parks depts (modeled from Carmel 500+ acres, Westfield Grand Park 400+ acres, Fishers and county parks)
metrics.note: Carmel alone has 500+ park acres; Westfield's Grand Park adds 400+ acres; substantial Fishers and county holdings. Blended estimate near the national median but with exceptional quality.

metrics.label: Residents within a 10-min walk of a park
metrics.source: TPL ParkServe (modeled for suburban form)
metrics.note: Dense park network in Carmel/Fishers cores raises walkable access above typical suburbs, though lower-density areas pull the county figure down from urban norms.

metrics.label: Playground quality
metrics.source: Field survey (surface, equipment age, upkeep)
metrics.note: Modern, well-maintained playgrounds with recent equipment and safety surfacing across Carmel, Fishers, and Westfield.

metrics.label: Library visits per capita
metrics.source: IMLS / Indiana State Library Public Library Statistics (Carmel Clay 5.86/1k-res-weighted; Hamilton East 3.31)
metrics.note: Carmel Clay Public Library ~5.86 visits per resident and Hamilton East ~3.31; both rank in Indiana's top 8 systems. County-blended ~4.5, well above the IMLS national median (~3.3).

metrics.label: Tree canopy
metrics.source: USFS NLCD Tree Canopy Cover (modeled from Indiana urban canopy assessment)
metrics.note: Mature established neighborhoods and strong municipal tree programs; estimated at ~30% for the developed county, higher in older Carmel/Noblesville tracts.

metrics.label: Learning rate
metrics.source: SEDA (modeled from Carmel Clay / Hamilton Southeastern proficiency rates)
metrics.note: Carmel Clay ~72% math / 73% reading proficiency and top-of-state performance imply above-average learning growth (>1.0 grade level per year).

metrics.label: Grade levels vs national average
metrics.source: SEDA (modeled)
metrics.note: Hamilton County's high-attainment, high-income districts (Carmel Clay, HSE, Zionsville-adjacent) test roughly 1.5-2 grade levels above the national mean.

metrics.label: Bachelor's degree or higher (age 25+)
metrics.source: Census ACS 1-year (2024) via Census Reporter
metrics.note: ±2.3%. Roughly double the national rate; among the most-educated counties in the Midwest.

metrics.label: Life expectancy
metrics.source: CDC/NCHS USALEEP / County Health Rankings
metrics.note: Highest of any Indiana county; residents live ~7 years longer than neighboring Marion County.

metrics.label: Child wellbeing
metrics.source: Modeled from CDC PLACES / County Health Rankings child poverty (4.5%)
metrics.note: Only ~4.5% of children in poverty (among the lowest in Indiana), strong schools and health access; composite modeled near the top of the scale.

metrics.label: Violent crime
metrics.source: FBI Crime Data Explorer (modeled; ~10.3 per 100k reported)
metrics.note: Reported violent-crime rate ~10.3 per 100k (about 1.0 per 1,000), less than half the national average of ~22.7 per 100k.

metrics.label: Drinking-water violations
metrics.source: EPA SDWIS (modeled; large well-run public utilities, Citizens Energy / Carmel Utilities)
metrics.note: Served by large, well-capitalized systems with no known significant health-based violations; estimated clean.

metrics.label: Broadband 100/20 Mbps availability
metrics.source: FCC Broadband Data Collection (modeled from provider coverage)
metrics.note: Dense suburban market with Spectrum, AT&T fiber, and fixed wireless covering ~99% of homes.

metrics.label: Gigabit availability
metrics.source: FCC Broadband Data Collection (modeled)
metrics.note: Spectrum gigabit and multi-gig fiber build-outs across Carmel/Fishers; NineStar CONNECT offers up to 10 Gbps in parts of the county.

metrics.label: Historic Register listings per 10,000 residents
metrics.source: National Register of Historic Places (modeled; historic Noblesville square, Cicero, older districts against large modern population)
metrics.note: A modest count of Register listings (Noblesville courthouse square, older townships) spread across a fast-grown suburban population of ~380k yields a low per-capita figure.

metrics.label: Cultural venues per 10,000 residents
metrics.source: IMLS Museum Universe / local (modeled; Palladium, Booth Tarkington Civic Theatre, Arts & Design District galleries, Nickel Plate District)
metrics.note: Rich venue density for a suburb: the Palladium / Center for the Performing Arts, Carmel Arts & Design District, Nickel Plate District amphitheater, and numerous galleries and theaters.

metrics.label: Town center quality
metrics.source: Field assessment (walkable town-center existence and quality)
metrics.note: Exceptional purpose-built walkable centers: Carmel City Center/Midtown and the Arts & Design District, Fishers' Nickel Plate District, and Noblesville's historic square.

metrics.label: Annual capital outlay per resident
metrics.source: Census of Governments / local ACFRs (modeled from Carmel, Fishers, Westfield capital programs)
metrics.note: Sustained high capital spend on roundabouts, trails, the Palladium, City Center, and Grand Park; per-capita outlay estimated well above the suburban norm.

metrics.label: Net capital assets per resident
metrics.source: Local ACFRs (modeled; net governmental capital assets across Carmel/Fishers/Westfield/county)
metrics.note: Large accumulated public infrastructure stock (roads, roundabouts, trails, arts and rec facilities) relative to population; estimated from municipal balance sheets.

metrics.label: Investment trend
metrics.source: Field assessment (is the place actively investing now?)
metrics.note: Actively building: ongoing roundabout program, Monon expansion, Midtown/Proscenium development, Fishers district growth, Westfield Grand Park. Among the most actively investing communities in the country.
